coredata.dc:title: A multi-target theranostic nano-composite against Alzheimer's disease fabricated by conjugating carbon dots and triple-functionalized human serum albumin
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2022 Acta Materialia Inc. Published by Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: Acta Materialia Inc. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Wenjuan
Lin, Xiaoding
Dong, Xiaoyan
Sun, Yan
coredata.dc:description:
The complex pathogenesis of Alzheimer's disease (AD) involves the aggregation and accumulation of amyloid β-protein (Aβ) as well as elevated levels of reactive oxygen species (ROS), which requires the development of comprehensive diagnostic and therapeutic interventions. In this work, a multifunctional theranostic nano-composite (HSA-BFP@CDs) is constructed by conjugating triple-functionalized human serum albumin (HSA-BFP) as a theranostic agent targeting Aβ and carbon dots (CDs) as an ROS scavenger. HSA-BFP@CDs exhibits a fluorescence “off-on” effect at 700 nm upon interaction with Aβ aggregates, showing the capability for detection of Aβ plaques and potential for early diagnosis of AD. Besides, HSA-BFP@CDs effectively inhibits the aggregation of Aβ, increasing the viability of Aβ-treated cells from 74% to over 95% at 100 µg/mL. Moreover, multiple ROS, including hydroxyl radicals, superoxide radicals, hydrogen peroxide, and Aβ-Cu2+-induced-ROS, can be scavenged by HSA-BFP@CDs, thus resulting in the mitigation of cellular oxidative damages. Experiments with the AD model of Caenorhabditis elegans further demonstrate the multifunctionality of HSA-BFP@CDs in imaging amyloid plaques, reducing Aβ deposition, and relieving oxidative stress in vivo, showing the prospect for Aβ- and ROS-targeted AD diagnosis and treatment. This work provided new insight into the design of protein-carbon dots conjugate and the development of multi-target therapy of AD.
Statement of significance Alzheimer's disease (AD) is the most common form of dementia, which currently affects over 55 million people worldwide. Due to the complex pathogenesis of AD involving amyloid β-protein (Aβ) aggregation as well as elevated levels of reactive oxygen species (ROS), it is highly desired to develop comprehensive diagnostic and therapeutic interventions. In this paper, we fabricated a multifunctional theranostic nano-composite (HSA-BFP@CDs) via the conjugation of triple-functionalized human serum albumin (HSA-BFP) and carbon dots (CDs). The multifunctionality of HSA-BFP@CDs for efficient detection of Aβ aggregates and inhibition of Aβ aggregation as well as scavenging of ROS was demonstrated, demonstrating the potential of the protein-carbon dots conjugate for the multi-target therapy of AD.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characteristics of the various agents. (a) TEM image (the inset presents size distribution and a high-resolution TEM image) of CDs, (b) size distributions of HSA-BFP and HSA-BFP@CDs analyzed by DLS. (c) 3D excitation-emission matrix fluorescence spectrum of HSA-BFP@CDs. XPS spectra of (d) HSA-BFP, (e) CDs, and (f) HSA-BFP@CDs. The high-resolution S 2p XPS spectra of (g) HSA-BFP, (h) CDs, and (i) HSA-BFP@CDs.

Fluorescence detection of Aβ fibrils. Fluorescence spectra of (a) HSA-BFP and (b) HSA-BFP@CDs upon interactions with different concentrations of Aβ aggregates (0–5 µM). The concentrations of HSA-BFP and HSA-BFP@CDs were both 30 µg/mL. Fluorescence imaging of Aβ deposits in AD nematodes with (c-e) HSA-BFP or (f-h) HSA-BFP@CDs. ThT (green emission) was used as an Aβ specific probe to stain the nematodes and was added with HSA-BFP or HSA-BFP@CDs (red emission) at the same time. Scale bars in (c-h), 50 µm. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Inhibition of Aβ fibrilization. (a) The normalized ThT fluorescence intensity of Aβ. Error bars correspond to standard deviations (n = 4). (b) AFM images. 1, Aβ only. 2, Aβ + 100 µg/mL HSA. 3, Aβ + 100 µg/mL CDs. 4, Aβ + 10 µg/mL HSA-BFP. 5, Aβ + 30 µg/mL HSA-BFP. 6, Aβ + 100 µg/mL HSA-BFP. 7, Aβ + 10 µg/mL HSA-BFP@CDs. 8, Aβ + 30 µg/mL HSA-BFP@CDs. 9, Aβ + 100 µg/mL HSA-BFP@CDs. (c) Far-UV CD spectra. Samples were prepared by incubating Aβ monomers (25 µM) without or with 30 µg/mL CDs, HSA, or HSA derivatives in PBS buffer at 37 °C for 120 h. (d) Viability of SH-SY5Y cells determined by MTT assays. Error bars represent standard deviations (n = 6): (###) p < 0.001 as compared to the control group. Values of p < 0.05 and p < 0.001 for the pairs of data sets are marked with ∗ and ∗∗∗, respectively. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Antioxidant capacity of HSA-BFP@CDs. (a) hydroxyl radical scavenging efficiency of different agents with various concentrations. (b) ·O2 − scavenging efficiency of HSA, HSA-BFP, CDs, and HSA-BFP@CDs at a concentration of 120 µg/mL. (c) Relative H2O2 level after mixing H2O2 with different agents. (d) UV–vis absorption spectra of KMnO4 (100 µM) exposed to HSA, HSA-BFP, HSA-BFP@CDs, and CDs at a concentration of 120 µg/mL.

The scavenging effect of HSA-BFP@CDs toward ROS induced by Aβ-Cu2+ in SH-SY5Y cells. Control: cells + PBS. Aβ and Cu2+concentrations were 25 and 10 µM, respectively. The concentrations of HSA-BFP, CDs, and HSA-BFP@CDs were 100 µg/mL. Scale bar, 50 µm.
Detoxification of HSA-BFP@CDs on Aβ-Cu2+-induced cytotoxicity. FDA-positive cells present live cells, while PI-positive cells present dead cells. Scale bar: 50 µm.

Detoxification of HSA-BFP@CDs on (a) H2O2 or (b) Aβ-Cu2+-induced cytotoxicity. The cell viability treated with PBS buffer only was set to 100%. Error bars represent standard deviations (n = 6): (###) p < 0.001 as compared to the control group. Values of p < 0.05, p < 0.01 and p < 0.001 for the pairs of data sets are marked with ∗, ∗∗ and ∗∗∗, respectively. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

HSA-BFP@CDs decreased Aβ deposition and alleviated Aβ-induced toxicity in AD nematodes. CL2006 at the L4 stage were incubated in the (a) absence or presence of (b) CDs, (c) HSA-BFP or (d) HSA-BFP@CDs for 3 d ThT (green emission) was used as an Aβ-specific fluorescence probe to stain all nematodes at the end of the incubation. Scale bar, 50 µm. (e) Survival curves of AD nematodes incubated with or without different agents. (f) ROS level of nematodes. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

head.ce:title: A multi-target theranostic nano-composite against Alzheimer's disease fabricated by conjugating carbon dots and triple-functionalized human serum albumin

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Biochemical Engineering, School of Chemical Engineering and Technology and Key Laboratory of Systems Bioengineering and Frontiers Science Center for Synthetic Biology (Ministry of Education), Tianjin University, Tianjin 300350, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The complex pathogenesis of Alzheimer's disease (AD) involves the aggregation and accumulation of amyloid β-protein (Aβ) as well as elevated levels of reactive oxygen species (ROS), which requires the development of comprehensive diagnostic and therapeutic interventions. In this work, a multifunctional theranostic nano-composite (HSA-BFP@CDs) is constructed by conjugating triple-functionalized human serum albumin (HSA-BFP) as a theranostic agent targeting Aβ and carbon dots (CDs) as an ROS scavenger. HSA-BFP@CDs exhibits a fluorescence “off-on” effect at 700 nm upon interaction with Aβ aggregates, showing the capability for detection of Aβ plaques and potential for early diagnosis of AD. Besides, HSA-BFP@CDs effectively inhibits the aggregation of Aβ, increasing the viability of Aβ-treated cells from 74% to over 95% at 100 µg/mL. Moreover, multiple ROS, including hydroxyl radicals, superoxide radicals, hydrogen peroxide, and Aβ-Cu2+-induced-ROS, can be scavenged by HSA-BFP@CDs, thus resulting in the mitigation of cellular oxidative damages. Experiments with the AD model of Caenorhabditis elegans further demonstrate the multifunctionality of HSA-BFP@CDs in imaging amyloid plaques, reducing Aβ deposition, and relieving oxidative stress in vivo, showing the prospect for Aβ- and ROS-targeted AD diagnosis and treatment. This work provided new insight into the design of protein-carbon dots conjugate and the development of multi-target therapy of AD.

Alzheimer's disease (AD) is the most common form of dementia, which currently affects over 55 million people worldwide. Due to the complex pathogenesis of AD involving amyloid β-protein (Aβ) aggregation as well as elevated levels of reactive oxygen species (ROS), it is highly desired to develop comprehensive diagnostic and therapeutic interventions. In this paper, we fabricated a multifunctional theranostic nano-composite (HSA-BFP@CDs) via the conjugation of triple-functionalized human serum albumin (HSA-BFP) and carbon dots (CDs). The multifunctionality of HSA-BFP@CDs for efficient detection of Aβ aggregates and inhibition of Aβ aggregation as well as scavenging of ROS was demonstrated, demonstrating the potential of the protein-carbon dots conjugate for the multi-target therapy of AD.

ce:section.ce:para: Alzheimer's disease (AD) is a progressive neurodegenerative disorder characterized clinically by the cognitive and memory impairment and pathologically by the accumulation of extracellular senile plaques and intracellular neurofibrillary tangles [1–3]. Elevated levels of amyloid β-protein (Aβ) are prone to aggregation from monomers into toxic oligomers and eventually form fibrous aggregates, which are the main components of senile plaques [4]. The aggregation of Aβ is considered to be one of the crucial factors contributing to neuron dysfunction and AD pathology. Therefore, Aβ species have become an important biomarker and target for the diagnosis and treatment of AD [5–7].

Great efforts have been made to develop potent AD theranostic agents for efficiently detecting Aβ species and inhibiting Aβ fibrillization, such as nanoparticles and small organic molecules [8–10]. Recently, we have developed a triple-functionalized human serum albumin (HSA-BFP) by conjugating Aβ fluorescent probe F and cell-penetrating peptide (Penetratin, Pen) onto basified HSA (HSA-B), and proved that HSA-BFP possesses high potency in detecting Aβ fibrils, inhibiting Aβ aggregation and penetrating blood-brain barrier (BBB) [11]. However, AD pathophysiology is complicated [12], and the inhibition of Aβ aggregation is not sufficient to effectively stop the AD progression. Oxidative damage caused by high levels of reactive oxygen species (ROS) is another pathological event in AD patients [13]. The excess ROS in the AD brain could be derived from impaired mitochondria in neurons or from Fenton-like reaction between metal ions and Aβ amyloid, and further exacerbate AD progression [14]. The complex pathophysiology of AD is enlightening the development of multifunctional agents that target multiple pathologies of AD, not just Aβ-targeted drugs.

Till now, several substances have been demonstrated to show high ROS scavenging activity, such as carbon- [15,16], cerium- [17,18], manganese- [19], and molybdenum-based nanomaterials [20]. Among them, carbon dots (CDs) that are mainly composed of carbon elements have attracted broad attention due to their good biocompatibility and valuable contributions to bioimaging, biosensors, and disease theranostics in diverse fields [21–23]. Wang et al. synthesized a new kind of CDs with broad-spectrum antioxidant properties from citric acid and glutathione (GSH) by a one-step hydrothermal method [24]. The CDs are able to efficiently scavenge multiple intracellular ROS and alleviate the lipopolysaccharide-induced inflammation, showing higher antioxidant activity than its precursor and great potential in oxidative stress-triggered diseases.

Herein, we proposed a hybridization design strategy to construct a multi-target nano-composite (HSA-BFP@CDs) via the conjugation of CDs with ROS-scavenging properties to HSA-BFP, the theranostic agent targeting Aβ reported by our group previously [11]. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first report on the fabrication and characterization of protein-carbon dots conjugate as a multifunctional nano-agent targeting AD. Extensive in vitro analyses and in vivo assays with Caenorhabditis elegans (C. elegans) were conducted to confirm the multifunctionality of HSA-BFP@CDs for efficient detection of Aβ aggregates and inhibition of Aβ aggregation as well as scavenging of ROS.

N-Succinimidyl-3-(2-pyridyldithio)propionate (SPDP), thioflavin T (ThT), HSA, 1,1,1,3,3,3-hexafluoro-2-propanol (HFIP), and 3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2yl)−2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT) were purchased from Sigma (St. Louis, MO, USA). Glutathione, copper chloride (CuCl2), and ferrous sulfate (FeSO4) were obtained from Biotechnology Engineering (Shanghai, China). Aβ40 (>95%, lyophilized powder) was from GL Biochem (Shanghai, China). Reactive oxygen assay kit, catalase assay kit, and superoxide radical's detection kit (WST-8) were received from Beyotime Biotechnology (Shanghai, China). The wild-type N2 strain and the transgenic CL2006 strain of Caenorhabditis elegans were obtained from the Caenorhabditis Genetics Center at the University of Minnesota (MN, USA). Human neuroblastoma cells (SH-SY5Y) were acquired from the Cell Bank of the Chinese Academy of Sciences (Shanghai, China). All other reagents were of analytical grade and obtained from local sources.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of CDs and HSA preparations
ce:section.ce:para: CDs were synthesized by a hydrothermal method according to Wang's et al. [24]. Briefly, citric acid (0.76 g) and glutathione (0.244 g) were dissolved in deionized water (20 mL). The solution was transferred to a Teflon-lined autoclave (50 mL) and heated at 120 °C for 12 h. After cooling to room temperature, the mixtures were dialyzed against deionized water with a 500 Da dialysis bag for 3 d Finally, the CDs were filtered with a Millipore filter (0.22 µM), lyophilized using a vacuum freeze drier (Labconco, MO, USA), and stored at −20 °C before use.

HSA-BFP was synthesized as reported previously[11]. HSA-BFP@CDs was fabricated by conjugating the CDs onto HSA-BFP with SPDP as a linker (Fig. S1). Firstly, HSA-BFP (110 mg, 1.3 µmol) was dissolved in 20 mL of 0.1 mol/L PBS (pH 7.4), and 210 µL SPDP stock solution (20 mM in DMSO) was dropped into the HSA-BFP solution. The reaction solution was kept at 25 °C and 170 rpm for 12 h to obtain HSA-BFP-SPDP. Then, HSA-BFP-SPDP (13 mg) and CDs (7 mg) were mixed with 6 mL of PBS and kept at 25 °C for 12 h to obtain HSA-BFP@CDs conjugate. The HSA-BFP-SPDP and HSA-BFP@CDs were both purified by dialysis, lyophilized, and stored at −20 °C.

The morphology and size distribution of CDs and HSA-BFP@CDs were observed by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (JEM-2100F, JEOL, Japan) with an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. The TEM image of HSA-BFP@CDs was obtained by negative staining with 2% (w/v) phosphotungstic acid (pH 7.4) and air-drying. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrum of CDs was detected with an FTIR spectrometer (Nicolet 6700, Thermo Fisher, USA). X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) of HSA-BFP, CDs, and HSA-BFP@CDs were measured with an X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (K-Alpha+, Thermo Fisher, USA). The fluorescence spectra of HSA, HSA-BFP, CDs, and HSA-BFP@CDs were determined with a fluorescence spectrometer (PerkinElmer LS55, USA). The hydrated size distribution of HSA-BFP and HSA-BFP@CDs were measured using a Zetasizer Nano instrument (Malvern Instruments, Worcestershire, UK). High-performance gel permeation chromatography (GPC) analysis of HSA, HSA-BFP, and HSA-BFP@CDs was performed on an Agilent 1100 Series HPLC (Agilent Technologies, CA, USA) using a TSKgel G3000SWXL column (fractionation range, 10−500 kDa. Na2SO4 (0.1 mol/L) in phosphate buffer (0.1 mol/L, pH 7.0) was used as an eluent solution.

Aβ40 powders were treated with HFIP to exclude existing amyloid fibrils and Aβ monomer stock solutions (275 µM in 20 mM NaOH) were prepared as reported previously [25]. Aβ aggregates were obtained by adding the Aβ monomer stock solution into PBS buffer (100 mM sodium phosphate, 10 mM NaCl, pH 7.4) to a final concentration of 25 µM and incubating at 37 °C and 150 rpm for 72 h.

HSA-BFP or HAS-BFP@CDs solutions (30 µg/mL in PBS) were mixed with different concentrations of Aβ aggregates (0–5 µM), and fluorescence emission spectra of HSA-BFP and HSA-BFP@CDs were detected at 500 nm excitation using a fluorescence plate reader (Infinite M200 Pro, TECAN, Salzburg, Austria). These samples were then observed by using an inverted fluorescence microscope (TE2000-U, Nikon, Japan).

Samples were prepared by mixing Aβ40 monomers and ThT without or with different concentrations of CDs, HSA, and HSA derivatives. The final concentrations of Aβ40 monomer and ThT were both 25 µM. Then, 200 µL of each sample was added to a 96-well plate and incubated in a TECAN fluorescence reader (Infinite M200 Pro, TECAN, Salzburg, Austria) at 37 °C. The fluorescence emission intensity of ThT at 480 nm (excitation at 440 nm) was recorded with a reading interval of 10 min and shaking for 10 s before each read. At least four replicates were performed for each group. The aggregation kinetics of Aβ was described by the ThT fluorescence curve fitted by the following equation [26],

Aβ40 monomer (25 µM) solution was incubated without or with CDs, HSA, or HSA derivatives at 37 °C for 120 h. Then, the sample (50 µL) was carefully dripped on a flat mica sheet for 1–3 min, followed by four times rinses with ultrapure water to wash away any salt ions. After air drying, the sample was observed using a multi-mode atomic force microscope (CSPM5500, Benyuan, China).

Samples were prepared by incubating Aβ40 monomers (25 µM) without or with 30 µg/mL CDs, HSA, or HSA derivatives in PBS buffer at 37 °C for 120 h. The CD spectra were measured using a CD spectrometer (J-810, Jasco, Japan) at a scanning speed of 100 nm/min. The CD spectrum of the solution containing only CDs, HSA, or HSA derivatives was subtracted as background.

MTT assay was conducted with SHSY-5Y cells as reported previously [25]. SH-SY5Y cells (80 µL,) were seeded in a 96-well plate at a density of 8000 cells/well and incubated at 37 °C with 5% CO2 for 24 h. The samples were prepared by incubating Aβ monomers (or Aβ-Cu2+) with or without 10–100 µg/mL of HSA, HSA-BFP, or HSA-BFP@CDs for 24 h, in which the concentrations of Aβ monomers and Cu2+ were 25 and 10 µM, respectively. The above samples (20 µL) were added into wells and incubated with cells for 24 h. Then, MTT (10 µL, 5.5 mg/mL in PBS buffer) was mixed with cells and incubated for another 4 h. The cell medium was centrifuged at 1500 rpm for 10 min, and the precipitated cells were re-dissolved with dimethyl sulfoxide. Last, the cell viability was determined by measuring the absorbance of each well at 570 nm. The viabilities of cells incubated with hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) (200 µM) mixed without or with 10–100 µg/mL of HSA, HSA-BFP, or HSA-BFP@CDs were also measured using the MTT assay. All of the experiments were performed with six replications. The cell viability was quantified by the following equation
(3)

Viability
( % )

×

D Control
where OD Control is the absorbance at 570 nm for the group with PBS buffer treatment and OD Treated is the absorbance at 570 nm for the group treated with different agents.

·OH was produced by the Fenton reaction between FeSO4 and H2O2 and measured according to the literature [27]. The thiobarbituric acid (TBA) reaction was used to determine the amount of ·OH, which degrades 2-deoxy-d-ribose to a 3-carbon compound (malondialdehyde) and leads to an absorbance at 532 nm. A volume of 100 µL of HSA, HSA-BFP, HSA-BFP@CDs, or CDs was prepared at different concentrations in PBS buffer. Then, a solution (50 µL) containing ascorbic acid (80 µM), 2-deoxy-d-ribose (20 mM), and FeSO4 (80 µM) were added. Next, 50 µL of H2O2 (880 µM) in PBS buffer was added to the above solution to initiate the Fenton reaction. After incubation at 37 °C for 90 min, the reaction solution was mixed with 100 µL of TBA solution (2% in 50 mM NaOH, w/v) and 100 µL of trichloroacetic acid (6%, w/v) and boiled for 15 min. After cooling down to room temperature and centrifugation, the absorbance of the supernatant was measured at 532 nm. The absorbance of solution without H2O2 was set as background and subtracted. Each experiment was repeated thrice.

The ability of HSA, HSA-BFP, HSA-BFP@CDs, and CDs to scavenge ·O2 − was determined by formazan formation using a superoxide radical's detection kit (WST-8) according to the manufacturer's instructions [28–30]. ·O2- was generated through the oxidation of xanthine by xanthine oxidase (XO) and then reacted with WST-8 to produce water-soluble formazan with characteristic absorption at 450 nm. The absorbance of each sample was calculated after subtracting the absorbance of the corresponding blank control.

The catalase-like activity of HSA, HSA-BFP, HSA-BFP@CDs, and CDs was determined with a catalase assay kit. Briefly, different concentrations of sample solutions (10–720 µg/mL) were treated with excess H2O2 (250 mM) for 5 min at 25 °C. After terminating the reaction, the remaining H2O2 was treated with peroxidase to generate the red product (N-(4-antipyryl)−3‑chloro-5-sulfonate-p-benzoquinonemonoimine). Then the absorbance at 520 nm was measured with a microplate reader (Infinite M200 Pro, TECAN, Salzburg, Austria). The absorbance of the PBS-treated group (control) was set to 100% to compare the relative levels of H2O2 after the treatment of different samples.

The antioxidant activity of HSA-BFP@CDs was evaluated by the KMnO4 reduction experiment [31]. Briefly, different agents (HSA, HSA-BFP, HSA-BFP@CDs, and CDs) at 120 µg/mL were separately mixed with acidified KMnO4 solution (pH 3, 100 µM), and incubated at 25 °C in the dark for 1 h. The absorbance of each mixture was then recorded from 400 to 700 nm with the microplate reader mentioned above. In the control group, pure water was used to replace the sample solution.

SH-SY5Y cells were seeded into 96-well plates with 8000 cells per well and incubated at 37 °C and 5% CO2 for 24 h. After the medium removal, SH-SY5Y cells were incubated with HSA-BFP, HSA-BFP@CDs, or CDs (100 µg/mL) for 3 h. Then SH-SY5Y cells were incubated with 20 µL of Rosup reagent (50 µg/mL) or Aβ-Cu2+ (25 µM Aβ and10 µM Cu2+) for 1 h, followed by staining by 2′,7′-dichlorodihydrofluorescein diacetate (DCFH-DA) for another 0.5 h in dark environment. SH-SY5Y cells without any treatment and treated with Rosup reagent or Aβ-Cu2+ were defined as negative control and positive control, respectively. Finally, the cells were washed carefully with PBS buffer and observed by fluorescence microscopy described above. The Rosup and DCFH-DA were obtained from the reactive oxygen assay kit.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescein diacetate (FDA) /propidium iodide (PI) double staining assays
ce:section.ce:para: A volume of 2 mL SH-SY5Y cells (100,000 cells/well) was added to a 6-well plate and incubated for 24 h. The cells were then stimulated with H2O2 (200 µM) or Aβ-Cu2+ (25 µM Aβ and10 µM Cu2+), followed by the addition of HSA-BFP, HSA-BFP@CDs, or CDs (100 µg/mL) to the SH-SY5Y cells for another 24-incubation. Before staining, the cell culture medium was removed, and 2 mL of FDA/PI solution was added into cells with FDA at 10 µg/mL and PI at 5 µg/mL in PBS buffer. After incubation at 37 °C for 15 min, the stained cells were washed five times with PBS and imaged with the fluorescent microscope mentioned above.
Two strains of C. elegans (N2 and CL2006) were cultured at 20 °C on the nematode growth medium (NGM) plates with E. coli OP50 as food [11,21].

For fluorescence imaging of Aβ deposits in the worms, adult worms were collected and fixed with 4% tissue fixative (paraformaldehyde) at 4 °C for 24 h. The fixative was then removed and the worms were stained with ThT (10 µM) and HSA-BFP/HSA-BFP@CDs (30 µg/mL) for 4 h. Last, the worms were washed three times with water and observed by fluorescence microscopy.

To investigate the effect of the administration of HSA-BFP@CDs on amyloid deposition in AD worms, the 4th stage larvae (L4) of CL2006 worms were transferred onto new NGM plates without or with HSA-BFP@CDs/HSA-BFP (100 µg/mL, 200 µL) and incubated for 72 h. The worms were then fixed with 4% tissue fixative, stained with ThT (10 µM), and observed by the fluorescence microscope as mentioned above.

To study the distribution of HSA-BFP@CDs in nematodes, CL2006 was incubated in the presence of HSA-BFP@CDs (100 µg/mL) for 0.5 and 4 h, and then fixed with 4% tissue fixative at 4 °C for 24 h. The distribution of HSA-BFP@CDs was visualized in the body of CL2006 by fluorescence microscopy (TE2000-U, Nikon, Japan).

The lifespan assay was conducted with CL2006 worms as reported previously[11]. Briefly, L4 worms were incubated on NGM plates (50 worms per plate) containing fluorodeoxyuridine (150 µM, 300 µL) in the absence or presence of HSA-BFP@CDs/HSA-BFP (100 µg/mL, 200 µL). The worms that showed no response to gentle touches with a platinum wire and had no pharyngeal motion were regarded as dead. The surviving worms were scored daily. Each group of survivors was removed to a new plate supplemented with corresponding agents for every 3 d.

The ROS level of C. elegans strain was measured by the DCFH-DA assay [32]. After incubating the L4 worms without or with HSA-BFP@CDs/HSA-BFP (100 µg/mL, 200 µL) for 3 d, the CL2006 worms were collected and stained with DCFH-DA (50 µM) for 2 h in the dark. Then, the fluorescence of worms was measured with excitation at 485 nm using a fluorescence reader (Infinite M200 Pro, TECAN, Salzburg, Austria). Sixty worms were analyzed for each experimental condition.
Data are presented as mean ± standard deviation (SD). All statistical analyses were performed by one-way ANOVA with Tukey's test, where p < 0.05 or less was considered statistically significant.

The TEM image of CDs in Fig. 1
a shows that the as-prepared CDs were well-dispersed with a size distribution range of 2–4 nm. The high-resolution TEM image (inset of Fig. 1 a) shows a planar spacing of 0.21 nm, corresponding to the (100) face of graphitic carbon [33]. The FTIR spectrum in Fig. S2 illustrates the functional groups of CDs. The strong absorption peak at 3440 cm−1 is attributed to the stretching vibration of O–H/N–H [34]. The peak at 1635 cm−1 is from the absorption of C=O bonds in COOH, CONH, CHO, and COSH [35]. The peaks at 1440 and 1080 cm−1 reflect the stretching vibrations of C=N and C=S bonds, respectively. The absorption peak at 1181 cm−1 is attributed to the C–O, C–N, and C–S bonds [24]. The FTIR results indicate the existence of abundant functional groups such as COOH, OH, NH2, and SH on the surface of the CDs. The morphology, particle size, and surface functional groups of CDs are in good agreement with the literature data [24].

The dynamic light scattering (DLS) results (Fig. 1 b) reveal the hydrated particle size of HSA-BFP (15.7 nm) and HSA-BFP@CDs (37.8 nm), indicating that the conjugation of HSA-BFP and CDs led to a protein-CDs composite with increased particle size. The TEM image in Fig. S3 further demonstrated the formation of the HSA-BFP@CDs composite, as depicted by the large light-colored circles (proteins) and the surrounding dark-colored dots (CDs) (the inset in Fig. S3). The GPC profile of HSA-BFP@CDs showed a clear shift to higher molecular weight as compared to HSA-BFP and HSA (Fig. S4), which is consistent with the DLS results.

The three-dimensional excitation-emission matrix fluorescence spectra (3D EEMS) of HSA, HSA-BFP, CDs, and HSA-BFP@CDs are shown in Fig. S5 and Fig. 1 c. Three fluorescence peaks located at the maximum excitation/emission wavelengths (Ex/Em) of nearly 280/340, 350/420, and 500/700 nm were observed in the 3D EEMS of HSA-BFP@CDs (Fig. 1 c), which were ascribed to the endogenous fluorescence of HSA (Fig. S5a), CDs (Fig. S5b), and the characteristic fluorescence of Aβ fluorescent probe molecule F (Fig. S5c), respectively. According to the standard curve of the concentration of HSA-BFP and its fluorescence emission intensity at 700 nm (Fig. S6), the conjugation quantity of CDs in HSA-BFP@CDs was calculated as 0.23 mg/mg, i.e., every 1 mg of HSA-BFP@CDs contained 0.23 mg of CDs and 0.77 mg of HSA-BFP.

The elemental composition and valence distribution of HSA-BFP, CDs, and HSA-BFP@CDs were investigated by the XPS survey (Fig. 1 d-i). As depicted in the XPS full scan spectra of HSA-BFP, CDs, and HSA-BFP@CDs (Fig. 1 d-f), these three were mainly composed of four elements: carbon, nitrogen, oxygen, and sulfur. Among them, CDs displayed the highest content of oxygen (30.69%) and sulfur (3.17%) (Fig. 1 e). Compared with HSA-BFP, HSA-BFP@CDs showed an increase in the contents of oxygen and sulfur to 19.81% and 1.23%, respectively. Moreover, the S 2p spectrum of HSA-BFP exhibited two peaks at 163.5 and 164.5 eV (Fig. 1 g), which are corresponding to S 2p3/2 and S 2p1/2 of C-S bonding [34,35]; while the S 2p spectra of CDs and HSA-BFP@CDs, in addition to the above-mentioned C-S bonding, also showed a signal peak at 168 eV (Fig. 1 h and 1 i), indicating that a small amount of S was presented as S=O bonds in the CDs and HSA-BFP@CDs [24].

The fluorescence property of HSA-BFP@CDs upon interaction with different concentrations of Aβ aggregates was investigated. Similar to the results for HSA-BFP (Fig. 2
a), the fluorescence intensity of HSA-BFP@CDs around 700 nm increased with the concentration of Aβ aggregates (Fig. 2 b), indicating that the fluorescence “turn-on” effect of HSA-BFP upon interaction with Aβ aggregates was retained after conjugation with CDs. The in vitro fluorescent staining experiments presented obvious red fluorescent spots after mixing HSA-BFP or HSA-BFP@CDs with Aβ aggregates (Fig. S7), further demonstrating the capability of HSA-BFP@CDs to fluorescently detect Aβ aggregates. The fluorescence response of HSA-BFP@CDs to Aβ aggregates was attributed to the conjugated F molecule, which would bring about enhanced fluorescence emission due to the restriction of intramolecular motions upon interaction with Aβ aggregates [11,36].

The transgenic C. elegans CL2006 expressing Aβ protein in the body muscle cells was then used as an AD model to study the fluorescence detection of Aβ plaques in vivo by HSA-BFP@CDs. ThT was used as a green fluorescent probe targeting Aβ to stain mature nematodes. From Fig. 2 c-h, significant green fluorescent spots of ThT-Aβ and red fluorescence signals from HSA-BFP or HSA-BFP@CDs were observed at the same sites in CL2006, confirming the colocalization of HSA-BFP/HSA-BFP@CDs and Aβ plaques. In the wild strain N2, which does not express Aβ, neither green nor red fluorescent spots were observed (Fig. S8). The fluorescent staining results of C. elegans demonstrated the promise of HSA-BFP@CDs for imaging amyloid deposits in vivo.

From the above results, it can be concluded that HSA-BFP@CDs maintained the fluorescence detection capability for Aβ aggregates both in vitro and in vivo. Compared with other Aβ probes, such as benzothiazolium derivative-capped silica nanocomposites (MSN-Lf@SZIs), carbazole-based cyanine modified superparamagnetic iron oxide nanoparticles (Fe3O4@SiO2@SLCONHR NPs), and lanthanide functionalized metal-organic coordination polymer (LMOCP), the detection ability of HSA-BFP@CDs on Aβ aggregates was not outstanding [7,37,38]. However, those probes were not capable of inhibiting Aβ fibrillization or scavenging ROS, which is what will be discussed next for HSA-BFP@CDs.

The inhibitory effect of HSA-BFP, CDs, and HSA-BFP@CDs on the aggregation of Aβ was assessed by ThT fluorescence assay, which is commonly used to quantify the β-sheet content in amyloid aggregates [39]. The aggregation kinetics and normalized ThT fluorescence intensities at the end of the test are shown in Fig. S9 and Fig. 3
a, respectively. The CDs at concentrations of 10–100 µg/mL had little effect on the aggregation of Aβ, as evidenced by its comparable lag time and fluorescence intensity with those of Aβ alone (Fig. S9a). HSA-BFP and HSA-BFP@CDs, however, significantly retarded the nucleation and fibrillization of Aβ in a dose-dependent manner and completely suppressed Aβ aggregation at 100 µg/mL, displaying much higher inhibitory efficacy than native HSA (Figs. 3 a and S9b-c). From the results, it can also be concluded that the conjugation of CDs had little effect on the inhibitory capability of HSA-BFP against Aβ fibrillization.

AFM was applied to visualize the morphology of Aβ aggregates formed in the absence or presence of different agents (Fig. 3 b). Aβ alone aggregated to densely entangled fibrous aggregates (Fig. 3 b1), consistent with previous reports [40]. After incubation with 100 µg/mL of HSA or CDs, substantial fibrils were still observed (Fig. 3 b2 and 3 b3), indicating that HSA and CDs at the concentration of 100 µg/mL could not inhibit Aβ fibrillization, consistent with the ThT result (Fig. 3 a). In contrast, the addition of HSA-BFP or HSA-BFP@CDs greatly blocked the Aβ fibrils formation. The formed fibrous aggregates were significantly reduced and shortened when Aβ was incubated with 30 µg/mL HSA-BFP or HSA-BFP@CDs (Fig. 3 b5 and 3 b8); moreover, the fibrillization of Aβ was completely suppressed in the presence of 100 µg/mL HSA-BFP or HSA-BFP@CDs, as no fibrous aggregates were observed (Fig. 3 b6 and 3 b9).

To get insight into the conformational transition of Aβ affected by HSA-BFP@CDs, CD spectroscopy was performed to analyze changes in the secondary structure of Aβ (Fig. 3 c). The CD spectrum of Aβ sample at 0 h showed a negative peak at 193 nm (Fig. 3 c, dash line), indicating that the secondary structure of Aβ monomers mainly presented a random coil [41]. After incubation for 120 h, Aβ displayed an intense negative valley near 216 nm and a positive peak near 192 nm (Fig. 3 c, black line), implying the formation of Aβ aggregates rich in β-sheet structures [41]. In the presence of HSA-BFP or HSA-BFP@CDs, however, the CD spectra of Aβ did not show the typical β-sheet structure, but maintained a distinct negative peak before 200 nm (Fig. 3c, red and blue lines), indicating that these Aβ still kept a random coil structure. The CD result confirmed that HSA-BFP@CDs effectively prevented the transition of Aβ to the β-sheet structure by stabilizing the Aβ structure in the same way as HSA-BFP.

MTT assay was further conducted with SH-SY5Y cells to evaluate the detoxification of HSA-BFP@CDs (Fig. 3d). HSA, CDs, HSA-BFP, and HSA-BFP@CDs had no evident effect on cell viability in the concentration range of 10–100 µg/mL (Fig. S10), suggesting the promising biocompatibility. Aβ aggregates showed significant cytotoxicity, inducing a decrease in cell viability to 74%. Compared to HSA and CDs, HSA-BFP and HSA-BFP@CDs exhibited higher potency in reducing the cytotoxicity of Aβ aggregates, elevating the cell viability to about 87% at 30 µg/mL and to over 95% at 100 µg/mL. The above results of ThT, AFM, CD, and MTT assays consistently demonstrated that both HSA-BFP and HSA-BFP@CDs could effectively inhibit the aggregation of Aβ and the resulting cytotoxicity. The Aβ species that were blocked from on-pathway aggregation may fail to trigger appreciable ROS production, thus leading to a comparable detoxification effect of HSA-BFP and HSA-BFP@CDs. Therefore, the antioxidant activities of HSA-BFP and HSA-BFP@CDs will be investigated next in detail with more extensive characterizations.

ce:section.ce:section-title: HSA-BFP@CDs scavenge ROS and protect cells against oxidative stress
ce:section.ce:para:
The scavenging activities of HSA, HSA-BFP, CDs, and HSA-BFP@CDs for ·OH were investigated by measuring their inhibition efficiency of the oxidation of 2-deoxy-d-ribose [27]. As shown in Fig. S11, the strong absorption at 532 nm corresponds to the oxidation of 2-deoxy-d-ribose by ·OH, which was generated from the Fenton reaction between FeSO4 and H2O2. The addition of HSA, HSA-BFP, CDs, and HSA-BFP@CDs reduced the absorbance at 532 nm to different degrees, revealing their different scavenging activities toward ·OH. (Figs. 4
a and S11). The CDs showed the best performance; nearly 72% of ·OH was scavenged by CDs at a concentration of 360 µg/mL, and CDs at 720 µg/mL completely cleared the generated ·OH. HSA and HSA-BFP exhibited weak antioxidant capacity as they only eliminated about 20% of ·OH at 720 µg/mL. By contrast, HSA-BFP@CDs was able to scavenge 26% of ·OH at 40 µg/mL and 54% of ·OH at 360 µg/mL, demonstrating the improved ·OH scavenging capacity of HSA-BFP@CDs as compared with HSA and HSA-BFP.

The ·O2 − scavenging efficiency of HSA-BFP@CDs was then evaluated via the xanthine oxidase method [42]. The ·O2 −generated from the oxidation of xanthine led to the formation of formazan with characteristic absorption at 450 nm. From Fig. 4 b, the absorbance intensity at 450 nm of the control group increased with reaction time, implying an increased production of ·O2 −. The addition of HSA-BFP@CDs (120 µg/mL) resulted in a significant decrease in the absorbance (49% at 30 min) as compared to the control group, showing a higher scavenging efficiency for ·O2 − than HSA and HSA-BFP.

To assess whether HSA-BFP@CDs could effectively scavenge H2O2, a catalase assay kit was applied to measure H2O2 levels after mixing H2O2 with different agents. As depicted in Fig. 4 c, HSA-BFP@CDs at 30 µg/mL reduced the level of H2O2 (250 mM) by more than 10% within a short time (5 min), whereas HSA and HSA-BFP lowered the H2O2 level by less than 5%, indicating that HSA-BFP@CDs exhibited stronger H2O2 scavenging activity than HSA and HSA-BFP. Further determination of the antioxidant activity of HSA-BFP@CDs was performed with the KMnO4 reduction assay, which is a typical method for evaluation of the reducing capacity of antioxidants [24]. Fig. 4 d shows that the characteristic absorption of KMnO4 (100 µM) at 525 nm was bleached in the presence of HSA, HSA-BFP, CDs, or HSA-BFP@CDs to varying degrees, confirming the reduction of KMnO4 to Mn2+, and HSA-BFP@CDs showed stronger reducing capacity toward to KMnO4 than HSA and HSA-BFP. According to Fig. 4 a-d, the total antioxidant capacity is ranked as CDs > HSA-BFP@CDs > HSA/HSA-BFP at the same mass concentration. The superior antioxidant activity of HSA-BFP@CD over HSA/HSA-BFP was attributed to the conjugation of CDs on HSA-BFP@CD with a modification degree of 0.23 mg/mg.

DCFH-DA, which can penetrate into the cell and react with intracellular ROS to produce the green fluorescent product dichloro-fluorescein (DCF), was applied to evaluate intracellular ROS levels [43, 44]. Fig. S12 shows that the introduction of Rosup, an ROS-generating agent, induced distinct green fluorescence signals in SH-SY5Y cells and led to a significant change in the cell morphology from polygonal to oval, suggesting that a large amount of ROS was produced and disrupted the structure of cells. HSA-BFP alleviated Rosup-induced ROS to some extent, but clear green fluorescence was still observed, while the addition of CDs and HSA-BFP@CDs significantly reduced the green fluorescence and restored the cells to normal morphology, indicating that CDs and HSA-BFP@CDs could effectively scavenge intracellular ROS and relieve oxidative damage of cells.

Dysregulated Cu2+ mediates the formation of highly cytotoxic Aβ-Cu2+ aggregates and leads to cellular oxidative damage through the generation of various ROS [45–47]. ROS production mediated by Aβ-Cu2+ has been widely investigated [48–50]. Aβ-Cu2+ could be reduced to Aβ-Cu+ and transfer an electron to oxygen (O2), yielding ·O2 − [49]. It can then form hydroperoxyl radical (·O2H) by electron/proton transfer and finally produce H2O2 [2]. Then, the resulting H2O2 can react with Aβ-Cu2+ or Aβ-Cu+ to generate ·OH or ·O2H via Fenton-like reactions [2,49]. Therefore, Aβ-Cu2+ could induce the production of dynamically variable ROS rather than a single ROS, and it is essential to investigate the scavenging efficiency of HSA-BFP@CDs toward ROS induced by Aβ-Cu2+ aggregates. As shown in Fig. 5 , after co-incubation of Aβ-Cu2+ with SH-SY5Y cells, visible green fluorescence was exhibited in the cells, indicating the formation of ROS. HSA-BFP@CDs decreased intracellular green fluorescence more significantly than HSA-BFP, confirming the enhanced potential of HSA-BFP@CDs to scavenge Aβ-Cu2+-induced ROS.

To further explore the function of HSA-BFP@CDs in protecting the cells from the damage of oxidative H2O2 and Aβ-Cu2+ aggregates, FDA/PI double staining experiment was conducted with SH-SY5Y cells (Figs. 6 and S13), where FDA and PI are fluorescent tracers for live (green) and dead (red) cells, respectively. The untreated SH-SY5Y cells in the control group presented only green fluorescence. Scattered red fluorescence signals (PI-positive staining) were observed after the treatment of H2O2 (Fig. S13) or Aβ-Cu2+ aggregates (Fig. 6), indicating the cytotoxicity of H2O2 and Aβ-Cu2+ aggregates. HSA-BFP had a slight influence on cell death caused by H2O2 or Aβ-Cu2+, while HSA-BFP@CDs effectively reduced the quantity of red fluorescence, suggesting that HSA-BFP@CDs attenuated the cytotoxicity of H2O2 and Aβ-Cu2+ on cells (Figs. 6 and S13). MTT assays further confirmed that HSA-BFP@CDs elevated the viability of H2O2-treated cells from 55.0% to 86.0% at 30 µg/mL and to 93.4% at 100 µg/mL (Fig. 7
a). Aβ-Cu2+ aggregates resulted in cell viability of 65.6%, exhibiting higher cytotoxicity than Aβ aggregates (see Figs. 3 d and 7 b), consistent with the literature [45]. HSA-BFP@CDs at 100 µg/mL increased the cell viability from 65.6% to 83.0%, which was significantly superior over HSA-BFP (70.5%, Fig. 7 b). The enhanced antioxidant capacity of HSA-BFP@CDs is supposed to facilitate the mitigation of oxidative damage by ROS formed in the Aβ-Cu2+ system.

Thus, with the potencies of imaging Aβ plaques, inhibiting Aβ fibrillogenesis, and relieving oxidative stress, HSA-BFP@CDs exhibited exciting promise for comprehensive diagnosis and multi-target treatment of AD, which presents advantages over other single-target agents, such as amphiphilic polyphenylene dendrons (APDs) and CLVFFA-functionalized gold nanoclusters [41,51]. The multi-target therapeutic properties of HSA-BFP@CDs will be demonstrated in vivo in the following section.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Inhibition of Aβ deposition and scavenging ROS in Caenorhabditis elegans
ce:section.ce:para:
To further examine the agents’ effects, AD transgenic nematodes (CL2006) were incubated with CDs, HSA-BFP, or HSA-BFP@CDs. In the ThT staining assays, the untreated CL2006 showed distinct green fluorescent spots in its body (Fig. 8
a), proving the aggregation and deposition of the amyloid in the CL2006 nematodes. Green emission spots were still observed in the nematode incubated with CDs (Fig. 8 b), indicating that CDs could not prevent Aβ aggregation; whereas CL2006 nematodes showed only uniform background fluorescence of ThT without obvious green spots after HSA-BFP or HSA-BFP@CDs treatment (Fig. 8 c and d), confirming the inhibition of amyloid accumulation in CL2006 by HSA-BFP or HSA-BFP@CDs.

The distribution of HSA-BFP@CDs in the body of CL2006 was visualized by fluorescence microscopy to further investigate the way of HSA-BFP@CDs penetrating into nematodes. As shown in Fig. S14, the red fluorescence from HSA-BFP@CDs was mainly distributed in the intestine, mouth, and cuticle of the nematode after incubating CL2006 with HSA-BFP@CDs for 0.5 h; while the red fluorescence was observed throughout the body after 4 h of incubation. Fig. S14 shows that HSA-BFP@CDs mainly entered the intestinal lumen of nematodes through their pharynx, followed by the distribution in the whole body; subsidiarily, it can also penetrate into the nematode through the cuticle (Fig. S14a-c), a weakly permeable membrane that acts as an exoskeleton and a barrier to environment [52]. Due to the high permeability and inhibitory capability of HSA-BFP@CDs, it could easily penetrate into the nematode, interact with Aβ expressed in body muscle cells, and inhibit the formation of fibrillar amyloid, thus potentially facilitating the metabolism of Aβ and decreasing the amyloid accumulation.

The aggregation and accumulation of Aβ in CL2006 is inclined to develop paralysis and death of the worms. From Fig. 8 e, the CL2006 nematodes without any treatment were completely paralyzed and died within 12 d HSA-BFP@CDs showed the best performance, significantly prolonging the lifespan of CL2006 to 18 d This may be because HSA-BFP@CDs not only inhibited the aggregation of Aβ and its toxicity but also decreased the ROS level induced by Aβ aggregates in CL2006 nematodes (Fig. 8 f). As a result, HSA-BFP@CDs alleviated Aβ-triggered toxicity and oxidation stress, thus achieving synergistic and multifunctional therapeutic effects.

In this work, we fabricated protein-carbon dots conjugate for the first time as a multifunctional theranostic nano-agent for Aβ- and ROS-targeted therapy of Alzheimer's disease and detection of in vivo amyloid formation. HSA-BFP@CDs exhibited high performance in the fluorescence detection of Aβ aggregates at 700 nm and inhibition of Aβ fibrillogenesis. Moreover, it presented effective antioxidant activities to scavenge various ROS, including ·OH, ·O2 −, H2O2, and Aβ-Cu2+-induced-ROS, thereby relieving the cellular oxidative damages. In vivo experiments demonstrated that HSA-BFP@CDs were effective in imaging amyloid plaques, reducing Aβ deposition and mitigating oxidative stress in AD nematodes, leading to a significant extension of lifespan. Thus, protein-carbon dots conjugates have been demonstrated promising for the development of potent agents against AD.
